skill.name: advanced-agentdb-vector-search-implementation
skill.description: Guides advanced AgentDB vector search implementations with QUIC replication, multi-database routing, custom metrics, hybrid search, performance tuning, and production monitoring.
skill.summary: Build and operate advanced AgentDB vector search systems for distributed AI workloads.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly documentation examples, not executable bundled code. Shell commands, network checks, alerting integrations, and environment-based secrets are real operational risks for users, while weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and obfuscation alerts are largely false positives from vector-search terminology and runbook text. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Deployment Examples Execute Shell and Package Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run npm installs, npx commands, shell scripts, node commands, and deployment tests. This is legitimate for a deployment guide, but users would execute third-party package lifecycle scripts and local deployment commands if they follow it directly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and repeated across the guide. Confidence is high that this is an operational execution risk, but the context is setup documentation rather than hidden execution.

medium_findings.title: Environment-Based Secret Configuration
medium_findings.description: The examples read environment variables for database hosts, Slack webhooks, PagerDuty keys, and SMTP credentials. This is a normal production configuration pattern, but it involves sensitive values and requires careful secret handling.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable access is directly present in example code. Confidence is high for sensitive configuration handling, but no evidence shows those secrets being sent to an unauthorized endpoint.

medium_findings.title: Network and Alerting Operations in Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes local health checks, curl-based runbook commands, SMTP email alerting, Slack webhooks, PagerDuty integration, and external documentation URLs. These are expected for monitoring, but they create network activity when implemented.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior is explicit in examples and runbook commands. Confidence is high for legitimate network operations, with no evidence of covert exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts appear to match vector-search and similarity terminology such as HNSW, hybrid similarity, and code similarity. The reviewed locations do not show weak hash algorithms or deprecated ciphers being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines are vector database configuration or similarity metric examples. Confidence is very high that these alerts are semantic false positives.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Operational Runbook Text
low_findings.description: Reconnaissance alerts map to health checks, ping troubleshooting steps, metrics checks, and diagram labels. These are normal observability and runbook instructions for a distributed database deployment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed evidence shows troubleshooting and architecture documentation, not hidden host discovery. Confidence is high, though users should still review commands before running them.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Paths Are Placeholders
low_findings.description: The production configuration shows certificate and key file paths for mutual TLS. The skill does not read or exfiltrate these files; it documents where production services might load them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are present only in configuration examples. Confidence is high that this is not direct credential access, but it remains sensitive operational guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: Markdown examples include commands that install packages, execute TypeScript, run deployment scripts, and invoke integration tests. Users should inspect packages and scripts before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell examples are clear and directly actionable. The risk depends on user execution and package trust, so confidence is high for the pattern but not for malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secrets Referenced Through Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code references credentials and webhook values through environment variables. This is preferable to hardcoding, but marketplace users should treat these examples as sensitive configuration paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly reference secret-bearing environment variables. Confidence is high that secret handling is relevant, with no confirmed misuse.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Calls for Health Checks and Notifications
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use fetch, curl, SMTP, Slack, and PagerDuty integrations for monitoring and alerting. These operations are legitimate but should be limited to trusted endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network operations are visible and fit the monitoring purpose. Confidence is high that the pattern exists, but medium-high on risk because endpoints are local or user configured.

content.value_statement: Distributed vector search systems are difficult to design, tune, and operate reliably. This skill provides a structured AgentDB workflow for replication, custom metrics, hybrid search, benchmarking, and monitoring.
actual_capabilities: Outlines a five-phase AgentDB deployment workflow from setup through production monitoring.
Shows multi-database replica configuration with QUIC synchronization concepts.
Defines custom distance metric patterns for weighted, hybrid, and domain-specific similarity.
Covers HNSW indexing, caching, quantization, and performance benchmark targets.
Provides operational examples for health checks, alerting, runbooks, and readiness validation.
limitations: The skill is documentation and example guidance, not a verified AgentDB implementation.
Some package names and APIs may require validation against the installed AgentDB version.
Production commands must be reviewed before use in real infrastructure.
Performance targets depend on hardware, data shape, index settings, and workload size.

use_cases.title: Design a Distributed Vector Search Cluster
use_cases.description: Plan primary and replica AgentDB nodes, routing, QUIC synchronization, and basic health checks.

use_cases.title: Tune Domain-Specific Similarity Search
use_cases.description: Create weighted and hybrid distance metrics that combine embeddings with metadata signals.

use_cases.title: Prepare Production Operations
use_cases.description: Draft monitoring, alerting, backup, runbook, and readiness checks for AgentDB deployments.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic AgentDB Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a simple AgentDB vector search setup with one primary database, two replicas, and health monitoring.

prompt_templates.title: Design Hybrid Search Metrics
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a hybrid search metric that combines vector similarity, metadata fields, and benchmark criteria for my dataset.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize a Production Workload
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to recommend HNSW, caching, quantization, and benchmark settings for a high-throughput AgentDB workload.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Full Deployment Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my AgentDB deployment plan for synchronization, failover, monitoring, alerting, backup, and operational risk.

output_examples.input: Plan a three-node AgentDB vector search deployment.
output_examples.output: A phased deployment plan covering primary setup, replica synchronization, routing, monitoring, validation checks, and operational handoff.

output_examples.input: Improve search relevance for code snippets and documentation.
output_examples.output: A metric design that blends embedding distance, syntactic similarity, semantic metadata, weighting rules, and benchmark success criteria.

output_examples.input: Prepare this AgentDB cluster for production.
output_examples.output: A readiness checklist for alerts, health endpoints, backups, runbooks, smoke tests, failover validation, and performance targets.

best_practices: Verify every package, command, and endpoint before running examples in a real environment.
Use secret managers or protected environment variables for credentials, webhooks, and service keys.
Benchmark with representative data before accepting latency, throughput, or accuracy targets.
anti_patterns: Do not run deployment scripts from examples without reviewing dependencies and side effects.
Do not hardcode credentials, webhook URLs, certificate paths, or production hostnames in shared code.
Do not assume quoted performance gains apply without measuring your own workload.

faq.question: Does this skill install AgentDB automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that users may adapt after reviewing commands and dependencies.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.

faq.question: Is the included deployment script production ready?
faq.answer: No. It is an example and should be reviewed, tested, and adapted for your infrastructure.

faq.question: Does it cover custom distance metrics?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes weighted, hybrid, and domain-specific similarity patterns.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: The documentation includes networked monitoring and alerting examples. Users control whether those examples are implemented.

faq.question: What is the main security concern?
faq.answer: The main concern is user-executed setup and deployment commands that depend on trusted packages, endpoints, and secrets.
